task: How do vector embeddings and vector databases work together to enable semantic search in a RAG system?

steps.input: vector embeddings semantic similarity sentence transformers
steps.reasoning: This is a multi-hop task — I need to understand both (a) how embeddings work and (b) how vector databases use them. Starting with embeddings.

tool_result.query: vector embeddings semantic similarity sentence transformers

results.title: Vector Databases and Embeddings
results.snippet: Text embeddings are dense vector representations (typically 384-1536 dimensions) that capture semantic meaning. Similar texts produce geometrically close vectors, measured by cosine similarity or Euclidean distance. Common embedding models include sentence-transformers (all-MiniLM-L6-v2). Vector databases use HNSW indexing for best speed-accuracy tradeoff.

results.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
results.snippet: During indexing, documents are split into chunks, converted to dense vector embeddings, and stored in a vector database. At query time, the question is embedded and used to search for the most similar chunks via approximate nearest-neighbour search.

results.title: NLP Evaluation Metrics
results.snippet: BERTScore uses contextual embeddings to measure semantic similarity beyond lexical overlap.

steps.input: HNSW FAISS approximate nearest neighbor search vector database indexing query
steps.reasoning: The first retrieval covered embeddings well. Now I specifically need information about how vector databases index and search those embeddings (HNSW, ANN search). Running a second targeted retrieval.

tool_result.query: HNSW FAISS approximate nearest neighbor search vector database indexing query

results.title: Vector Databases and Embeddings
results.snippet: Indexing strategies: flat (exact search, O(n) complexity); IVF (inverted file index, clustering-based approximate search); HNSW (hierarchical navigable small world graphs for fast approximate nearest neighbour search). HNSW offers the best speed-accuracy tradeoff for most production use cases.

results.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
results.snippet: RAG depends heavily on chunking quality and index accuracy. The user's question is embedded and used to search for the most similar document chunks.

results.title: MLOps: Model Deployment and Operations
results.snippet: REST APIs for standard inference; specialized LLM servers with batching and KV-cache optimization.

steps.input: How do vector embeddings and vector databases work together to enable semantic search in a RAG system?
steps.reasoning: I now have comprehensive information about both embeddings (doc_007, doc_006) and vector database indexing strategies (doc_007 second retrieval). I can synthesize a complete multi-hop answer.
steps.tool_result:
Here is how embeddings and vector databases work together in a RAG system:

**Step 1 — Create embeddings (indexing time)**
Each document chunk is passed through an embedding model (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2). The model converts the text into a dense vector of 384 floating-point numbers. Semantically similar texts produce vectors that are geometrically close — 'machine learning' and 'deep learning' will be near each other; 'pizza recipe' will be far away.

**Step 2 — Store in a vector database**
These vectors are stored in a vector database (e.g., ChromaDB, FAISS, Weaviate) using an efficient index structure. The most common is HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World), which organises vectors in a graph so similar vectors are connected. This allows sub-linear search time (much faster than checking every vector).

**Step 3 — Query (retrieval time)**
When a user asks a question, the same embedding model converts the question into a query vector. The vector database performs approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) search — it traverses the HNSW graph to find the top-k stored vectors that are closest (by cosine similarity) to the query vector.

**Step 4 — Return semantically relevant chunks**
The returned chunks are semantically related to the question — not just keyword-matched. A query for 'how does attention work?' correctly retrieves chunks about 'self-attention mechanism' even if the phrase 'how does attention work' never appears in the document.

This is what makes RAG semantic rather than keyword-based: meaning is encoded into geometry.

final_answer: Embeddings convert text into dense vectors where similar texts are geometrically close. Vector databases (HNSW index) store these vectors and enable sub-linear approximate nearest-neighbour search. At query time, the question is embedded and the database returns the top-k semantically similar chunks — enabling meaning-based retrieval, not just keyword matching.
